content:
I am a pre-final year undergraduate student at the Computer Science Department at BITS Pilani, Pilani Campus. I am deeply interested in robotics, specifically in the area of motion planning. I am also exploring new areas related to deep learning, and am actively exploring reinforcement learning.
I am currently working with Prof. Avinash Gautam on a multi-robot path planning project, exploiting the formation of clusters from frontiers for optimal coverage of the environment. I am also developing my computer vision skills while working on anamoly detection in videos. This project is being done under the supervision of Prof. Navneet Goyal. I spent my last summer with the A.R.M.S. Lab at IIT Bombay headed by Prof. Arpita Sinha, exploring the navigation stack for a tractor trailer robotic system. I have previously worked on a ML based person state classification project at CEERI Chennai under the mentorship of Dr Madan Kumar Lakshmanan.
I am always looking forward to new opportunities!
summary: I am a pre-final year undergraduate student at the Computer Science Department at BITS Pilani, Pilani Campus. I am deeply interested in robotics, specifically in the area of motion planning. I am also exploring new areas related to deep learning, and am actively exploring reinforcement learning.
title: Srujan Deolasee

title: Example Talk

content:
e-Yantra Robotics Competition eYRC is an international competition organized by IIT Bombay. I led a student team of four and participated in the 2020-21 edition of the competition. Our theme focussed on developing software for a 3‑D simulated quadcopter capable of package delivery in a cityscape Gazebo world. It was titled “Vitarana Drone”, literally translating to “delivery drone”. The competition spanned around six months from October 2020 to March 2021, and consisted of continuous task based evaluations with increasing difficulty and complexity. We had to use Python and ROS to develop all the software.
My team comprised of three other members- Atharva Chandak, Ayush Agrawal, and Naitik Khandelwal. All of us were from BITS Pilani.
We were provided with the Gazebo model of the drone and the world. The drone package had the relevent topics for controlling the PWM of the four motors. Apart from that, we had to develop all the code needed to autonomously move the drone, right from scratch. The drone carried various sensors like IMU, GPS, Camera for marker detection (localization near the packages to be picked up or delivered), and four range sensors at perpendicular directions.
We employed a dual and discrete PID based controller, each for the attitude and the position of the drone. The attitude controller was cascaded in the position controller. All the values for proportional, integral, and derivative constants were obtained by using Plotjuggler and some trial and error. The attitude controller was tuned first, and after freezing its values, we tuned the position controller. IMU values (roll-pitch-yaw) were used as a feedback for the attitude controller, and GPS was used as a feedback for the position controller.
After making the controller good enough for our use, we had to develop a path planner. We were only provided with the initial location of the drone. We were given a setpoint of a marker, which was near the final goal (landing station). Once we reached the setpoint, we had to search for the landing station using marker detection, and accordingly land inside the regulated area. Since we did not have access to any map of the world, we employed a real time planning algorithm and used a variant of the bug 0 algorithm. We developed the code from scrath, and added a functionality such that the drone moves along the m-line (joining the initial location and the final setpoint) in a discrete manner. The term discrete is used as we divided the m-line into small sections of 5 meters, and the the drone moved along these smaller sections in a start-stop manner while hovering momentarily. Thus the m-line was divided into a path of many setpoints. As soon as an obstacle was detected using any of the range sensors, a new setpoint was calculated in the direction perpendicular to that of the range sensor which detected the obstacle. After moving towards the new setpoint, the same procedure was repeated and the m-line was modified such that the new starting position was the latest calculated set point. Note that the set point might have been inside some obstacle boundary, but the drone never collided as the range sensor in that direction would have detected the obstacle and a new setpoint would have been calculated in the perpendicular direction.
Once the planner was complete, we had to figure out a marker detection algorithm. While the “detection” of the marker was easily done using OpenCV, the location of the marker was unknown. So what we did was, once the drone reached the given setpoint which was near the marker, it went up in order to increase the area scanned by the camera. Once the marker was detected, it came down and went near the marker and landed successfully inside a rectangular boundary denoting the regulated area.
After the contoller and the planner was developed and integrated successfully, we had to incorporate the package lifting and delivering mechanism using an attatched gripper. The gripper was controlled using an automated ROS Service, which needed to be called by us through our code. Since the package itself had some weight, the controller was initially failing to carry it successfully without toppling. We sorted out this issue by tuning the controllers again, with the difference being that the drone was carrying the package in this case. Since the gripper was controlled using a service, we had a way to know if the drone was carrying a package currently. Thus, whenever it wasn’t carrying a package, we used the PID parameter values we had figured out initially, and switched to the new values whenever a parcel was detected. Thus, our drone could now successfully deliver packages.
The final task of the competition involved optimising the number of drones delivered in a given amount of time. A reward was calculated using the distance travelled by the drone and the nnumber of packages delivered successfully, and the effective reward was used to rank the teams. We were given the locations of the setpoints near all the parcels (which had a marker so that it could be detected using the marker-detection algorithm I mentioned above), and the delivery locations of the parcels. We needed to write a scheduling algorithm in order to find the order in which we could deliver packages and increase the reward.
Finally, we were ranked in the top 20 of the 450+ participating teams. Although we did not win the competition, it was a very valuable experience and helped all of us develop our problem solving, critical thinking, and coding skills.
summary: Developed software for a simulation-based package delivery drone.
title: e-Yantra Robotics Competition

content:
GenNav, short for ‘General Navigation’, was undertaken by the Electronics and Robotics Club at BITS Goa. As we did the project during the COVID-19 lockdown, I remotely contributed to the open-source project.
GenNav is a python library for robotics navigation algorithms and utilities commonly required in navigation stacks. The library aims to be completely modular and have a unified API so that it is usable in a broad range of applications and easily extensible to new robotics domains. We further extended the project to construct a ROS Wrapper on top of Gennav, called ‘gennav_ros’. ‘gennav_ros’ can be used if someone wishes to use ‘gennav’ in a ROS based stack. Since the package is open-sourced using PyPi here, GenNav can also be installed using pip install gennav, although installation from source is recommended. Rudimentary documentation for the project can be found here.
As part of the team, I implemented path planning algorithms like Rapidly‐exploring Random Tree (RRT), Rapidly‐exploring Random Graph (RRG), and some utility classes and methods for implementing Graphs, etc. While implementing the algorithms from scratch was challenging (albeit fun), the more important thing to take care of was maintaining the API across all the path planners and making all generalised pieces of code ‘modular’. I also made Jupyter and Google Colab notebook tutorials for showing how to use the available algorithms. The notebooks can be found here.
summary: Built a general navigation library in python.
title: GenNav

content:
The project was based on the study of various feature detectors and image matching algorithms applied on the forehead dataset. As a part of Deep Learning course, I built a computer vision based forehead based human authentication on mobile and hand held devices. The project covers the use of SIFT, SURF, ORB, ArcFace, and AKAZE algorithms for matching images. Image Preprocessing (CLAHE) was performed before applying the matching algorithms. I achieved an average accuracy of 59.99%, and best accuracy of 73.997% using the ArcFace algorithm. A genuine-imposter histogram was plotted for each algorithm. The generated genuine and histogram datasets were used to plot the receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve of FRR (X-Axis) vs FRR (Y-Axis). Correct Recognition Rate (CRR) and Equal Error Rate (EER) were calculated from the resulting ROC curve.
P.S.: This project was done as a part of the Deep Learning course offered at BITS Pilani. The detailed report of the project can be found here.
summary: Studied of various feature detectors and image matching algorithms applied on the in-house forehead dataset
title: Human Authentication System using Forehead Wrinkles

content:
This is a project based on path planning and control theory of robots. What this means is that I need to plan a path from the given start to goal points through a given environment using one of the path planning algorithms. I then need to create a code for a controller for the robot. This will enable the robot to traverse the path in a ‘point-by-point’ manner to reach the goal. The environment in which my project runs consists of a series of static obstacles and the robot. Trotbot (a robot model) has been used to test my code. Trotbot is based on the Omnibase package. Both are developed by the Electronics and Robotics Club, BITS Goa.
I implemented a PID controller and a Randomly Exploring Random Tree (RRT) path planner for a Trotbot robot. The Trotbot autonomously planned its way through a series of static obstacles and traversed the resultant path. The code was written using Python, and ROS was used as the framework. Each entity with different functionality (e.g.: controller, path planner) was written as a ROS Node. The Publisher-Subscriber, or Pub-Sub, model in ROS was used to set up the communication between the nodes.
Structure of the project:
 Obstacle detector node which publishes a fixed list of obstacles. Here I published the list of centre points of the cylindrical obstacles. Planner node which subscribes to obstacles and publishes a path. Controller node which subscribes to the path and the current pose of the robot. It calculates the required velocity of the robot, and publishes it on the corresponding topic. This controller will traverse the path one point at a time using PID Controller.  All the code was tested on the robot using simulations on Gazebo. More information about the project can be found here.
P.S.: This project was done as a part of the QSTP Course Project. QSTP (Quark Summer Term Projects) is an annual event where the technical clubs from BITS Goa offer introductory courses in their respective domains. My introduction to robotics was this very project, offered in May 2020 during the COVID-19 lockdown. The course page can be found here. Since it was my first door to the vast field of robotics, this project holds a special position in my heart. I’ll always be grateful to the course mentors, whose efforts are the reason I have come this far in robotics.
summary: Developed software for a robot to plan and traverse a obstacle free path autonomously.
title: Path Planner and Autonomous Controller for a Robot

content:
A strictly two-wheeled robot (without a free castor wheel for support) provides a helpful platform for testing complex control algorithms. It helps ensure that a multi-variable system will be physically stable. In this project, we study the use of the modern Linear Quadratic Regulator controller for balancing the robot and extend the application for the robot to traverse any path. A differential drive mechanism is used while developing the robot, which helps decouple the translation and rotation stages in path traversal. An A* algorithm has been used for path planning in a static obstacle occupied environment. It is shown that an LQR controlled robot can successfully help the robot stabilize on the spot without falling and enable linear motion in the forward direction.
P.S.: This project was done as a part of the Autonomous Mobile Robotics course offered at BITS Pilani. The project report can be found here.
summary: Developed software for a self-balancing mobile robot using on two standard wheels.
title: Self Balancing two wheeled robot

Create slides in Markdown with Wowchemy Wowchemy | Documentation
 Features  Efficiently write slides in Markdown 3-in-1: Create, Present, and Publish your slides Supports speaker notes Mobile friendly slides   Controls  Next: Right Arrow or Space Previous: Left Arrow Start: Home Finish: End Overview: Esc Speaker notes: S Fullscreen: F Zoom: Alt + Click PDF Export: E   Code Highlighting Inline code: variable
Code block:
porridge = "blueberry" if porridge == "blueberry": print("Eating...")   Math In-line math: $x + y = z$
Block math:
$$ f\left( x \right) = ;\frac{{2\left( {x + 4} \right)\left( {x - 4} \right)}}{{\left( {x + 4} \right)\left( {x + 1} \right)}} $$
 Fragments Make content appear incrementally
{{% fragment %}} One {{% /fragment %}} {{% fragment %}} **Two** {{% /fragment %}} {{% fragment %}} Three {{% /fragment %}}  Press Space to play!
One  Two  Three 
 A fragment can accept two optional parameters:
 class: use a custom style (requires definition in custom CSS) weight: sets the order in which a fragment appears   Speaker Notes Add speaker notes to your presentation
{{% speaker_note %}} - Only the speaker can read these notes - Press `S` key to view {{% /speaker_note %}}  Press the S key to view the speaker notes!
 Only the speaker can read these notes Press S key to view    Themes  black: Black background, white text, blue links (default) white: White background, black text, blue links league: Gray background, white text, blue links beige: Beige background, dark text, brown links sky: Blue background, thin dark text, blue links    night: Black background, thick white text, orange links serif: Cappuccino background, gray text, brown links simple: White background, black text, blue links solarized: Cream-colored background, dark green text, blue links   Custom Slide Customize the slide style and background
{{< slide background-image="/media/boards.jpg" >}} {{< slide background-color="#0000FF" >}} {{< slide class="my-style" >}}   Custom CSS Example Let’s make headers navy colored.
Create assets/css/reveal_custom.css with:
.reveal section h1, .reveal section h2, .reveal section h3 { color: navy; }   Questions? Ask
